coredata.dc:title: Accelerating sodium-ion electrode material development through AI-driven optimization and predictive modeling
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Alzaabi, Sara
Elkamel, Ali
Karanikolos, Georgios N.
Alhammadi, Ali
coredata.dc:description:
Sodium-ion batteries (SIBs) are gaining traction as a cost-effective and sustainable alternative to lithium-ion batteries for large-scale energy storage, due to sodium’s abundance, low cost, and safety advantages. However, the discovery of high-performance electrode materials for SIBs remains a significant challenge because of the complex interactions between compositional and structural features that govern key properties such as specific capacity, average voltage, and volume change. In this study, we present an artificial intelligence (AI)-driven framework that integrates machine learning and multi-objective optimization to accelerate the design of sodium-ion battery electrodes. Four predictive models, namely Decision Tree, Random Forest, Support Vector Machine (SVM), and Deep Neural Network (DNN), were trained on a feature-rich dataset derived from high-throughput computational databases. The DNN model achieved the highest predictive accuracy, with R
2
values up to 0.97 and mean absolute errors (MAE) below 0.11 for the target properties. To support material selection, the DNN was coupled with the Non-dominated Sorting Genetic Algorithm II (NSGA-II) to identify Pareto-optimal materials that maximize specific capacity while minimizing volume expansion. The resulting candidates exhibit balanced electrochemical performance and potential for practical SIB applications. This study demonstrates the power of combining deep learning and optimization to guide the discovery of next-generation energy storage materials with high efficiency and reduced experimental overhead.
coredata.openaccessSponsorName: Khalifa University 2024: Hybrid and Gold Journals (Prepaid Pot)
coredata.dcterms:subject: Artificial intelligence
Electrode materials
Machine learning
Sodium-ion batteries

Comparison and evaluation of machine learning models

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Khalifa University of Science and Technology through the project number RC2-2018-024 . The authors would also like to acknowledge the significant contributions of Khalifa University\u2019s high-performance computing and research computing facilities. These resources have been crucial in obtaining the results presented in this research.

head.ce:title: Accelerating sodium-ion electrode material development through AI-driven optimization and predictive modeling

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Sodium-ion batteries (SIBs) are gaining traction as a cost-effective and sustainable alternative to lithium-ion batteries for large-scale energy storage, due to sodium’s abundance, low cost, and safety advantages. However, the discovery of high-performance electrode materials for SIBs remains a significant challenge because of the complex interactions between compositional and structural features that govern key properties such as specific capacity, average voltage, and volume change. In this study, we present an artificial intelligence (AI)-driven framework that integrates machine learning and multi-objective optimization to accelerate the design of sodium-ion battery electrodes. Four predictive models, namely Decision Tree, Random Forest, Support Vector Machine (SVM), and Deep Neural Network (DNN), were trained on a feature-rich dataset derived from high-throughput computational databases. The DNN model achieved the highest predictive accuracy, with R
2
values up to 0.97 and mean absolute errors (MAE) below 0.11 for the target properties. To support material selection, the DNN was coupled with the Non-dominated Sorting Genetic Algorithm II (NSGA-II) to identify Pareto-optimal materials that maximize specific capacity while minimizing volume expansion. The resulting candidates exhibit balanced electrochemical performance and potential for practical SIB applications. This study demonstrates the power of combining deep learning and optimization to guide the discovery of next-generation energy storage materials with high efficiency and reduced experimental overhead.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Transitioning to clean energy involves harnessing the potential of renewable energy resources, such as wind and solar power, while also adopting emission-free transportation methods. However, to make the most of renewable energy, it is essential to have appropriate technologies for converting and storing energy. This is necessary to address the intermittent energy production and to support clean transportation demands [1]. Electrochemical energy storage methods have proven to be the most efficient and versatile solution, catering to diverse applications in both stationary and mobile storage systems [2]. A balance between both power and energy densities has caused lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) to be the leading energy storage system [3]. Batteries have brought remarkable advancements in portable devices concerning their performance, lifespan, weight, and dimensions [4]. Up until now, LIBs have commanded significant attention from both the research community and industries. Nevertheless, increasing interest is being directed towards exploring alternative technologies that leverage sodium ions as primary charge carriers. This surge in interest is mainly attributed to the limited availability of lithium precursor materials, as well as the geopolitical sensitivity surrounding their geographical distribution [3]. Adding to the issue is the projected increase in LIB demand, which is likely to drive a significant rise in battery costs in the future [5]. While recycling used lithium is being considered as a viable option, the practicality of relying on lithium precursors and the environmental impact associated with their extraction continue to be points of contention [6].

Sodium-ion batteries (SIBs) have emerged as a promising and sustainable alternative to LIBs for large-scale energy storage. Unlike lithium, sodium is abundantly available, geographically widespread, and cost-effective, making it a strategic choice for countries lacking lithium reserves. While sodium has a larger ionic radius and higher atomic mass, which lead to somewhat lower theoretical energy densities, recent progress in material engineering has narrowed the performance gap between SIBs and LIBs, particularly in terms of power capability and cycling stability. SIBs store energy through the reversible intercalation and deintercalation of Na
+
ions between layered anode and cathode materials during charging and discharging cycles. This mechanism is similar to LIBs but presents unique challenges due to sodium’s size and coordination behavior, which influence structural changes in electrode materials. Despite this, SIBs exhibit enhanced safety, thermal stability, and operational robustness, making them particularly well-suited for grid-scale storage, backup systems, and renewable energy integration. Importantly, their reliance on more sustainable and less geopolitically constrained resources supports the global push for decarbonized and resilient energy systems [7,8].

Battery research and development imposes a multifaceted challenge as it incorporates various aspects such as safety, life-cycle analysis, performance, cost, environmental considerations as well as resource concerns. Presently, research often follows a forward trial-and-error methodology, primarily focused on materials exploration. This encompasses activities such as material synthesis, electrolyte and electrode manufacturing, cell assembly, and subsequent performance evaluation. Yet, even when considering these facets alone, the potential avenues for synthesizing active materials and preparing electrolytes amount to more than
1
0
100
possibilities, with an almost limitless range of choices for electrode manufacturing parameters and various cell formats [9]. This complexity surpasses human cognitive capacity, posing challenges for predictive inverse design tools that could foresee battery component properties needed to achieve specific performance targets and cell configurations [9]. Furthermore, batteries and other electrochemical devices pose unique challenges for materials design due to the incomplete understanding of structure–property relationships for crucial attributes. Moreover, batteries inherently consist of different materials interacting unpredictably or uncontrollably. This difficulty is amplified by the fact that experimental validation of potential materials or designs often spans weeks to months due to various factors, including the complexity of solid-state material synthesis, quantum mechanical simulations on extensive time and length scales, and the necessity of lengthy cell-level performance tests involving hundreds to thousands of cycles. As a result, battery datasets are often confined to a relatively small number of samples, ranging from mere tens to hundreds [10]. Artificial Intelligence (AI) has shown significant promise in accelerating battery materials research and development by addressing key challenges, such as managing complex variable interactions and processing large datasets [9].

In recent years, AI has revolutionized battery research, providing substantial advancements in material design, characterization, manufacturing, and diagnostics. Most studies conducted are fairly recent (2017–2024) which shows that this field is emerging. Notable examples include the use of random forest models to analyze operational descriptors affecting SIB discharge capacity and life cycle [11], neural networks to estimate state-of-charge [12], and unsupervised learning for real-time performance prediction [13]. Another notable example is the work by Chen et al. [14] which introduced an innovative methodology for materials discovery that harmoniously blends AI techniques with cloud-based high-performance computing (HPC) to expedite the design and identification of novel materials. In their research, the team leveraged cutting-edge AI models alongside traditional physics-based models, harnessing the computational power of cloud HPC to sift through over 32 million material candidates. Remarkably, this rigorous screening process identified approximately half a million potentially stable materials within a span of less than 80 h, facilitated by the deployment of around one thousand virtual machines (VMs) in the cloud. However, a sequential elimination process was employed, applying one criterion at a time to eliminate candidate materials. This approach fails to consider the entire picture holistically, as it focuses on filtering for specific properties, which can lead to suboptimal solutions. Although these advancements have been significant, challenges and research gaps need to be addressed to further advance the field of energy storage technology. For instance, enhancing computational techniques or refining AI models could decrease the dependency on computationally demanding methods such as Density Functional Theory (DFT). These improvements could expedite the materials discovery process and broaden its accessibility to researchers lacking extensive computational resources.

The performance of batteries is predominantly evaluated based on specific metrics such as voltage and specific capacity, which collectively contribute to the overall energy density [15,16]. Additionally, attributes like Coulombic efficiency, cyclability, and safety are intrinsically linked to the volume changes occurring within electrode materials during charge and discharge cycles [17]. Traditionally, the exploration of these volume changes has been approached through time-intensive methods, including DFT simulations and empirical experimentation [18]. Hence, there is an imperative need to develop more efficient and accurate methodologies for studying and predicting electrode material volume changes. Such advancements can significantly enhance the battery design and performance evaluation processes, ultimately driving innovation in energy storage technologies. Given the aforementioned challenges and opportunities, the primary objective of this paper is to develop advanced AI-based models capable of predicting the average voltage, volume change, and specific capacity of various battery combinations. The subsequent focus shifts towards the development of an optimization framework in which the AI model is employed as a pivotal tool to propose and recommend property adjustments that optimize the performance metrics of battery electrodes.

This section outlines the methodology adopted for predicting the key performance metrics of battery electrodes, which encompasses data collection, processing and featurization, and model building. The methodology is visually shown in the flowchart of (Fig. 1), which delineates each step from data collection to model evaluation, providing a clear and structured overview of the entire process. Such systematic approach was needed as to ensure the development of robust and accurate predictive models.

The dataset for this study was obtained from the work of Moses et al. [18], who sourced their data from the Materials Project (MP) database [19]. Since the MP database contains only 4351 intercalation electrodes out of which only 309 have sodium as their working ion, Moses et al. [18] developed an algorithm that gathers all the solid state structure materials (129,397 crystalline solid materials) and searches for potential discharged and charged electrode pairs. The metals ions searched for were Li, Na, Cs, Y, Ca, Mg, Zn, Al and Rb. As a result, 191,625 instances of charged and discharged electrode combinations. The dataset included the identification number (ID) of the materials used for the cathode and anode, the working ion, the average voltage (
V
av
), the volume change percentage during charging and discharging (
Δ
V
%
), the specific energy (SE), and the specific capacity (C). For new electrode combinations
V
av
,
Δ
V
%
, C and SE were calculated using the following equations respectively [18]:
(1)
V
a v
= −
E
discharged
−
E
charged
−
(
x
2
−
x
1
)
E
M
q
(
x
2
−
x
1
)
(2)
Δ
V
%
=
|
V
discharged
−
V
charged
|
min
(
V
discharged
,
V
charged
)
× 100 %
(3)
C =
n × F × 1000
3600 ×
M
M
(4)
S E = C ×
V
a v

E
discharged
and
E
charged
represent the total energies of the electrode after discharge and the corresponding charged electrode, respectively.
E
M
denotes the energy per atom of the active metal in its pure elemental state.
x
1
and
x
2
refer to the quantities of metal ions in the charged and discharged electrodes, respectively. The variable q represents the charge transferred by the active metal, which can be monovalent, divalent, etc.
V
discharged
and
V
charged
stand for the volumes of the discharged and charged electrodes. n is the number of electrons transferred, F represents Faraday’s constant, and
M
M
indicates the molar mass of the discharged electrode. An important note is that the dataset contains negative voltage values; this is crucial for training a robust model that can predict if a material is unsuitable for use as an electrode.

Using the Materials Application Programming Interface (MAPI) [20] and the Python Materials Genomics (pymatgen) [21], the chemical formulas of the cathode and anode materials were queried. The dataset was then refined, and duplicates were removed. After this processing, 128,561 electrode combinations were left.

In order to build a machine learning model that predicts the required properties of metal-ion batteries, the chemical composition of each battery electrode must be uniquely represented. To achieve this, the Matminer software [22] was utilized. Matminer is an open-source Python software library designed for feature extraction, offering 47 featurization classes capable of generating thousands of material property descriptors and their mathematical relationships. The chemical formulas of the electrode pairs were processed using Matminer to extract 329 features, and further categorized into four groups, adapted from [23]:

ce:list-item.ce:para: Stoichiometric features, relying solely on the fractions of constituent elements rather than their specific identities. This encompasses factors such as the count of elements within the compound and various Lp norms derived from these fractions.

Statistics of the elemental properties, encompassing measures like the mean, absolute deviation, range, mode, minimum, and maximum of the values of the properties of each element present in the material. It includes 22 distinct elemental characteristics. This category entails attributes like the maximum row on the periodic table, the average atomic number, and the range of atomic radii across all elements present in the substance. The mean and average deviation are calculated using:
(5)
f
̄
=
∑
i
x
i
f
i
where
f
i
is the property of element i ,
x
i
is the atomic fraction, and
f
ˆ
is the average deviation.
Attributes related to the electronic structure, quantifying the average proportion of electrons from the s, p, d, and f valence shells among all constituent elements.

Ionic compound attributes, encompassing evaluations of the feasibility of forming an ionic compound when assuming that all elements possess a single oxidation state. Additionally, two adaptations of the fractional ’ionic character’ of a compound are included, which are established through an electronegativity-based metric. The electronegativity difference between the two elements is calculated using:
(6)
I
(
X
A
,
X
B
)
= 1 −
e
− 0 . 25
(
X
A
−
X
B
)
2
where I represents the fraction of ionic character,
X
A
is the electronegativity of element A, and
X
B
is the electronegativity of element B.

ce:section.ce:para: Since nine of the features are categorical, one-hot encoding was performed to transform these features into a binary format suitable for machine learning. This resulted in a total of 22,059 features, where 21,714 represent the one-hot encoded categorical features and 318 represent the numerical features. Given the diverse magnitudes of these features, the standard scaler method from Scikit-learn [24] was applied to normalize each feature by removing the mean and scaling to unit variance.

High feature counts can lead to overfitting, causing models to excel on training data but falter on new data. Moreover, high-dimensional spaces confront the “curse of dimensionality”, becoming computationally demanding and time-consuming. To address these challenges, it is crucial to evaluate each feature’s impact on model performance. A permutation importance analysis (PIA) was conducted using the Histogram Gradient Boosting Regressor model for each parameter. Fig. 2 displays the top 10 most influential features for specific capacity, average voltage, and volume change separately. The selected features are:

ce:list-item.ce:para: Active Metal Atoms Discharge (AMAD) The number of atoms of active metals participating in the discharge process. These active metal atoms typically undergo redox reactions during battery discharge, thus directly affecting how much charge can theoretically be delivered. While a higher number of active metal atoms generally increases the potential number of electrons transferred (and thus total available charge), it is important to recognize that specific capacity (capacity per unit mass) also significantly depends on the atomic or molecular weight of the material. Therefore, materials with a high count of active metal atoms but higher molecular weight may exhibit lower practical specific capacities [25].

Active Metal Atoms Charge (AMAC) The number of atoms of active metals involved in the charging process. During charging, active metal atoms (e.g. sodium ions in SIBs) migrate back to the anode. The efficiency and number of redox-active atoms in the cathode are key determinants of how much energy the battery can store. In sodium-ion batteries, where the sodium source (anode) is typically in excess, it is the cathode’s capacity to reversibly accommodate sodium ions that largely governs the total energy storage. This affects the maximum voltage the battery can achieve when fully charged and influences the total energy stored, thus impacting the specific capacity [25].

Average Covalent Radii Charge (ACRC) The average size of the covalent radii of the atoms in the charging electrode. The average covalent radii of atoms in the charging electrode influence the structural and electronic properties of the material. Larger or more uniform covalent radii can enhance the structural stability of the electrode, reduce internal stresses during ion insertion/extraction, and improve ionic conductivity. These factors contribute to more efficient charge storage and retrieval, affecting the voltage stability and the capacity of the electrode [26,27].

Fraction p Electrons Discharge (FPED) The fraction of p-valence electrons of the discharging electrode material. The fraction of p-valence electrons determines the type of chemical bonding and the electronic properties of the discharging electrode material. p electrons are involved in covalent bonding and can influence the material’s electrical conductivity and redox behavior. Materials with a higher fraction of p electrons may exhibit better electronic conductivity and more favorable redox potentials, leading to enhanced performance during discharge, higher voltage, and better capacity retention [28,29].

Mode Electronegativity Discharge (MED) Mode electronegativity of the discharging electrode material. Electronegativity measures an atom’s ability to attract and hold onto electrons. The mode electronegativity of the discharging electrode material indicates the most common electronegativity value among the atoms present. Materials with a suitable electronegativity balance ensure efficient electron transfer during discharge, reducing overpotentials and improving the overall energy efficiency of the battery. This influences the discharge voltage profile and the overall capacity of the electrode [30].

Fraction s Electrons Charge (FSEC) The fraction of s valence electrons of the charging electrode material. s electrons are typically involved in metallic bonding and can influence the conductivity and reactivity. A higher fraction of s electrons in the charging electrode material can lead to better electrical conductivity and facilitate the movement of ions and electrons during the charging process. This enhances the electrode’s ability to accept and store charge, affecting the specific capacity and the efficiency of the charging process [28,29].

Average Deviation Covalent Radii Discharge (ADCRD) The average deviation of the size of the covalent radii of the discharging electrode material. The average deviation in covalent radii among the atoms in the discharging electrode material affects the uniformity of the electrode structure. High deviation can lead to structural irregularities and create localized stress points, hindering ion diffusion and electron transport. This can increase internal resistance, reduce the efficiency of redox reactions, and negatively impact the discharge voltage and capacity [31].

Average Column Number Charge (ACNC) Average column number (atomic number) of the atoms in the charging electrode. The average column number corresponds to the average atomic number of elements in the charging electrode, which reflects the overall metallic character and valence electron configuration. Elements with higher atomic numbers may have more complex electron configurations and can contribute to a range of redox potentials and bonding scenarios. This diversity can affect the electrode’s capacity to store and release charge, influencing the voltage and specific capacity of the battery [32,33].

Range Column Number Discharge (RCND) Range of the column number (atomic number) of the atoms in the discharging electrode. The range of column numbers indicates the diversity of elements and their electronic properties in the discharging electrode. A wider range suggests a variety of atomic sizes and bonding characteristics, which can introduce complexity in the electrode’s redox behavior and structural stability. This diversity can impact the uniformity of discharge reactions and the overall electrochemical performance, affecting the voltage and capacity [34,35].

Max Column Number Discharge (MCND) Maximum column number (atomic number) of the atoms in the discharging electrode. The maximum column number gives insight into the heaviest element present in the discharging electrode. Heavier elements (with higher atomic numbers) often have unique electronic structures and redox properties that can significantly influence the electrode’s behavior. These elements can introduce high-capacity redox couples or unique structural attributes that enhance the electrode’s performance during discharge, impacting the voltage stability and specific capacity [34,35].

ce:section.ce:para: These features are chosen for their explanatory power across all three parameters. The objective is to minimize the number of features while maintaining acceptable accuracy, enabling the model’s broader applications and expediting the discovery of new materials without overwhelming data requirements. Subsequently, a SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) analysis [36] is performed with the best-performing model to further investigate each feature’s relative influence on the model’s output by comparing their effects against the average. The findings are discussed in the results section.

Various AI models were employed to predict specific capacity, average voltage, and volume change in metal-ion batteries. Initially, standard machine learning methods such as decision trees, random forests, and support vector machines (SVM) were utilized, with a random state of 42 for decision trees and random forests. The SVM used the Radial Basis Function Kernel (RBF) [37], a stationary nonlinear kernel. Subsequently, DNN models were constructed for each parameter.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Decision tree model and interpretability analysis
ce:section.ce:para: To improve interpretability, the hierarchical structure of the decision tree model was examined in the context of average voltage prediction by conducting a feature importance analysis as illustrated in Fig. 3. The initial split was determined by the working ion, highlighting its dominant influence on intercalation behavior and voltage response. Subsequent splits were based on the mean and minimum Mendeleev numbers, capturing periodic trends that differentiate materials by elemental composition. The charging formula further refined the partitions by incorporating stoichiometric and structural information, while the maximum atomic weight contributed additional compositional insight. These decision points illustrate how the model sequentially filters materials using chemically meaningful descriptors, enabling both predictive accuracy and design relevance.

A DNN is a machine learning algorithm that is inspired by the human brain. It consists of multiple nodes interconnected with each other where each of them contains a small piece of information. The nodes are divided into layers where the first layer is the input layer, the middle layers are the hidden layers, and the last layer is the output layer. The input layer consists of nodes equal to the number of features and the output layer consists of one node per output. As for the hidden layer, the number of nodes should be optimized based on the problem. The training procedure begins when the input nodes are directed to the hidden layer, each node in the hidden layer is equal to the sum of the value of each node of the previous layer multiplied by a coefficient called “weight”, which is adjusted during the training. The sum of the weighted inputs is then used as an input in the activation function to produce the neuron’s output. The activation function introduces nonlinearity in the model which allows it to capture more complex relationships in the data. The output of each neuron becomes the input of the neuron in the next layer. This process continues throughout the layers until it reaches the output layer. Once the final outputs are found, they are compared with the actual values using a loss function. After that, the error is used to adjust the weights of each layer through the backpropagation of the loss computed using gradient descent. This entire process is repeated until the network’s performance converges to a satisfactory level. The term “deep” in deep neural networks refers to the depth of the architecture, which means it includes multiple hidden layers. DNNs can learn complex representations from data which makes them more suitable for handling complex tasks and high-dimensional data [30].

The DNN model architecture used comprises dense, batch normalization, activation, dropout, and output layers. The input shape is set to (None, 10), accommodating 128,562 samples with 10 features each. The model has 3201 parameters, 3009 of which are trainable. Key components include two dense layers with 64 and 32 neurons and an output layer with a single neuron. To prevent overfitting, batch normalization, a dropout layer with a rate of 10, and L2 regularization in the first and second layers were employed. The Rectified Linear Unit (ReLU) activation function was chosen for its ability to introduce non-linearity, capturing complex data relationships [38]. The Root Mean Squared Propagation (RMSprop) optimizer was utilized, employing a stochastic mini-batch learning method. Mean Squared Error (MSE) served as the loss function, and Mean Absolute Error (MAE) as the evaluation metric. The model parameters are summarized in Table 1, detailing the settings for the capacity and volume change models and the voltage model.

The dataset was split into training (80%) and testing (20%) subsets using a random state of 42. The training set was further partitioned into training (80%) and validation (20%) subsets, maintaining the same random state. This setup ensures that 80% of the data is used for training while the remaining 20% is reserved for testing. Additionally, the DNN model incorporates a 5-fold cross-validation mechanism during training and validation.

To assess model performance, Mean Absolute Error (MAE) was utilized due to its robustness against outliers, which is crucial given the dataset’s negative voltage values. MAE quantifies the average magnitude of errors between predicted and actual values. Additionally, the coefficient of determination (R-squared) was calculated to gauge the model’s goodness-of-fit and explanatory power. This metric aids in assessing predictive accuracy.

In this subsection, a summary of the models developed to predict specific capacity is presented. Table 2 provides a summary of the performance metrics on the testing data for each model. The decision tree achieved an MAE of 9.649 and an R
2
value of 0.987, demonstrating strong predictive capability. The random forest model increased MAE slightly to 9.824 but improved R
2
to 0.992, indicating enhanced accuracy. In contrast, the SVM model showed a higher MAE of 124.06, suggesting reduced accuracy despite a moderate R
2
of 0.721. The DNN model outperformed all others with an impressive MAE of 0.109 and an R
2
of 0.974, showcasing superior predictive performance and ability to capture complex relationships.

The DNN model exhibited the best predictive accuracy for specific capacity, followed by the random forest model, while SVM and decision tree models showed comparatively weaker performance. The learning curve of the DNN model (Fig. 4(a)) demonstrated effective learning initially, with both training and validation errors decreasing. Subsequently, validation error fluctuated while training error continued to decrease, indicating potential overfitting. Regularization techniques like dropout and L2 regularization were employed to mitigate overfitting.

Despite some divergence between training and validation errors, the DNN model maintained strong predictive performance on test data, reflected in low MAE and high R
2
values. A scatter plot evaluation (Fig. 5(a)) further illustrated the model’s predictive accuracy, with points closely aligned along a diagonal line indicating accurate predictions. An R
2
value of 0.974 quantitatively affirmed the model’s ability to explain 97.4% of specific capacity variability, underscoring its robust predictive capability and reliability for metal-ion battery research.

This subsection discusses the results of volume change percentage prediction. Table 3 provides a comparative analysis of predictive performance across four models. The DNN model demonstrates superior performance with an impressive MAE of 0.117 and an R
2
value of 0.845, indicating it captures approximately 84.5% of volume change variance effectively. In contrast, the SVM model performs poorly with a high MAE of 190.269 and a low R
2
value of 0.0413. The Random Forest and Decision Tree models show intermediary performance, with the Random Forest slightly outperforming the Decision Tree. Overall, these findings underscore the DNN model’s superior predictive capability for volume change compared to other models evaluated.

The learning curve of the DNN model (Fig. 4(b)) reveals insights into its training and validation performance across epochs. Initially, both training and validation errors decrease as the model learns. However, validation error fluctuates as training progresses, occasionally dropping below training error. This variability suggests challenges in generalizing to unseen data points, despite overall improvement in predictive capability with each epoch.

A scatter plot evaluation (Fig. 5(b)) further illustrates the DNN model’s predictive accuracy for volume change. The R
2
value of 0.845 quantifies alignment between predicted and actual values. Although this value is slightly lower than the R
2
of other target properties, it still indicates a strong predictive correlation in a materials science context. Given the inherent variability in structural deformation data, particularly across chemically diverse materials, this level of accuracy is sufficient for guiding material selection and multi-objective optimization efforts. This robust predictive capability suggests that the DNN model is reliable for volume change prediction in batteries.

Table 4 presents a comparative analysis of predictive performance for average voltage among various models. Initially utilizing 10 features, the DNN shows reasonable performance with an MAE of 0.2807. Recognizing the need for enhanced prediction, additional features such as capacity and volume change were incorporated, resulting in significant improvement. The MAE decreases impressively to 0.0783, accompanied by an R
2
value of 0.893, showcasing the model’s robustness and ability to leverage additional information effectively.

The learning curve (Fig. 4(c)) provides insights into training and validation performance across epochs. Both training and validation errors decrease as the model learns, though validation error remains slightly higher and fluctuates, indicating challenges in generalization to unseen data.

A scatter plot (Fig. 5(c)) compares DNN predicted values to true values for voltage prediction, demonstrating alignment with an R
2
value of 0.893, signifying strong predictive performance. This underscores the DNN’s ability to capture underlying patterns and relationships in data, making it a reliable predictor for average voltage in metal-ion batteries.

SHAP (Shapley Additive Explanations) is a robust framework in explainable AI, aimed at interpreting machine learning model predictions [36]. This study applied SHAP to interpret DNN model predictions across specific capacity, volume change, and average voltage.

Fig. 6(a) illustrates SHAP analysis on the specific capacity DNN model, highlighting AMAD and FPED as significant features positively influencing predictions. Low AMAD values negatively impact specific capacity, while high AMAC values negatively influence predictions. ACRC, ADCRD, and MCND exhibit variable impacts depending on their values.

Fig. 6(b) depicts SHAP insights into the volume change model, emphasizing AMAD and MED as positive predictors, while ACRC and AMAC negatively affect predictions. These features maintain consistent importance across capacity and volume models.
Fig. 6(c) presents SHAP analysis on the average voltage model, showing gravitational energy and specific capacity as influential features with varying impacts.

In summary, among the features analyzed, AMAD consistently showed high importance across all machine learning models, particularly in predicting specific capacity. This descriptor represents the number of metal atoms involved in redox reactions during the discharge process, which directly correlates with the number of electrons transferred per formula unit. In intercalation-based systems like sodium-ion batteries, this determines how much charge the cathode can accept from sodium ions, making AMAD a strong predictor of capacity. Furthermore, higher AMAD values often indicate more redox-active sites, enhancing the material’s ability to store and release energy. The model’s emphasis on AMAD aligns with fundamental electrochemical theory and supports its validity as a key design parameter.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison and evaluation of machine learning models
ce:section.ce:para: To systematically evaluate the machine learning approaches employed in this study, a comparative analysis was conducted to assess their performance, interpretability, and suitability for predicting electrode material properties.

The decision tree model offers strong interpretability by enabling direct visualization of decision rules based on input features. In this work, the model successfully identified key descriptors, such as Charging Active Metal Atoms and Mean Mendeleev Number, as primary determinants of electrode performance. However, it exhibited limited generalization capability and a tendency to overfit, particularly on small datasets. Its predictive performance was comparatively lower than that of ensemble and deep learning models.

Random forest builds upon individual decision trees through ensemble averaging, which leads to improved robustness and predictive accuracy. It demonstrated reliable performance across all three target properties and showed resistance to overfitting. However, the model’s interpretability is reduced due to the distributed nature of decision logic across numerous trees. Additionally, its computational cost is higher compared to single-tree methods.

SVM models are theoretically well-suited for capturing nonlinear relationships via kernel transformations. In this study, however, the SVM model yielded higher prediction errors and lower R
2
values than other models. Its sensitivity to hyperparameter tuning and difficulty scaling to larger datasets limited its practical effectiveness for the given application.

The DNN model outperformed all other models in terms of accuracy for specific capacity, average voltage, and volume change. Its ability to learn complex nonlinear mappings from a wide range of features makes it especially well-suited for high-dimensional data. Although interpretability remains a challenge, the use of SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) provided insights into feature importance, which helped mitigate this limitation.

In summary, the DNN model is recommended for tasks that require high predictive accuracy in sodium-ion electrode material screening. Nevertheless, decision trees and random forests offer valuable interpretive insights into feature influence and model behavior. Future directions may involve combining the interpretability of tree-based models with the predictive strength of deep learning through hybrid or ensemble architectures. The integration of machine learning models with multi-objective optimization in this study provides actionable insights for the design of sodium-ion battery electrodes. Specifically, the most influential descriptors identified, such as AMAD, average covalent radius, and atomic weight deviations, suggest that high specific capacity is associated with a greater number of redox-active sites and favorable atomic-scale uniformity. Conversely, minimal volume change correlates with reduced covalent radius mismatch and enhanced structural compactness. These findings indicate that electrode materials composed of redox-active elements with moderate atomic weights, uniform covalent sizes, and stable crystal frameworks are more likely to achieve an optimal trade-off between energy density and mechanical stability. Such model-derived insights can assist experimental researchers in pre-screening material candidates and tailoring synthesis conditions to prioritize compositions with balanced electrochemical performance and structural integrity.

Advancing energy storage technologies requires optimizing key battery metrics such as volume and capacity to develop efficient, cost-effective, and environmentally friendly solutions. Tailoring batteries for specific applications, whether high capacity for electric vehicles, compact size for wearables, or high performance for renewable storage, enhances flexibility across industries. This section details the application of the Non-dominated Sorting Genetic Algorithm II (NSGA2) for multi-objective optimization [39].

To address the challenge of balancing volume change and specific capacity in battery design, this study formulates the problem as a multi-objective optimization, aiming to minimize volume change while maximizing specific capacity:
(7)
Minimize Δ
V
%
,
Maximize C

The search space for optimization is defined by the range of feature values derived from scaled battery data, encompassing the minimum and maximum values for each feature. The NSGA2 algorithm was selected for this task due to its effectiveness in handling multi-objective optimization problems. The algorithm is configured with the following parameters:

ce:list-item.ce:para:
Population size:
N = 100
Sampling: Real random sampling

Crossover: Simulated Binary Crossover (SBX) with probability
p
c
= 0 . 9
and eta
η
c
= 15

Mutation: Polynomial Mutation with eta
η
m
= 20

Reference Directions: Energy-based reference directions with
M = 100
points

ce:section.ce:para: The NSGA2 algorithm evolves a population of candidate solutions towards the Pareto front over 100 generations. These parameters and methods are carefully selected to balance exploration and exploitation to efficiently find the Pareto-optimal solutions for the battery optimization problem.

The objective functions for the optimization are defined as follows:
(8)
Δ
V
%
=
f
volume DNN model
(
x
10 features
)
(9)
C = −
f
capacity DNN model
(
x
10 features
)
where the DNN models are used to predict the capacity and volume change. The capacity is negated since the model minimizes by default. The search space, derived from the scaled dataset, ensures coverage of all feasible feature values, while constraints are imposed to prevent negative predictions of volume change percentages, maintaining realistic and physically meaningful results.

The NSGA2 algorithm yields a 2D Pareto front (Fig. 7), illustrating trade-offs between volume and capacity. The visualization highlights the best compromises between these two objectives, providing insights into how volume and capacity influence each other. This multi-objective approach demonstrates NSGA2’s effectiveness in balancing key performance metrics and identifying Pareto-optimal solutions.

A case study was conducted using the developed optimization framework to pinpoint solutions tailored to specific performance needs across various energy storage applications. In particular, grid energy storage systems (GESS) are pivotal in modern energy infrastructure, providing reliable, flexible, and efficient electricity supply [40]. With the increasing integration of renewable energy sources into power grids, effective storage solutions become essential for balancing variable generation and managing demand. This case study centers on optimizing GESS performance through advanced computational methods, offering insights into the features that most significantly influence system functionality. Although specific material recommendations fall beyond the current scope, the study illuminates key parameters essential for efficient energy storage, such as specific capacity, volumetric expansion, and additional material properties that affect GESS reliability and performance.
GESS optimization adheres to specific requirements:

ce:list-item.ce:para: Volumetric Expansion Tolerance: Up to 30% during charge/ discharge cycles [41].
Specific Capacity: Higher values crucial for efficient energy storage [42].

ce:section.ce:para: In line with these requirements, the optimization process has identified four potential solutions, as illustrated in Fig. 8. The optimization results reveal a spectrum of specific capacities and volume change percentages, indicating the inherent trade-offs in GESS design. Each solution represents a unique combination of features, and understanding their individual and collective impacts is crucial for GESS development.

Furthermore, the identification of optimal trade-offs between capacity and volume allows for more efficient material selection and design processes, minimizing the need for resource-intensive trial-and-error methods. These insights contribute to the development of cost-effective, high-performance batteries suited to diverse industry needs without compromising reliability.

In the context of renewable energy integration, the results reveal configurations that effectively address the challenges of intermittent energy sources, such as solar and wind. High-capacity, volume-stable batteries from the Pareto front are particularly beneficial for storing surplus energy in renewable farms, thereby ensuring consistent energy availability in line with fluctuating generation patterns.

The study’s findings also support applications in grid stability and resilience, demonstrating battery designs that endure high-stress charge/discharge cycles. This feature is vital for load balancing, ensuring continuous energy supply during demand spikes and ultimately enhancing grid reliability. Additionally, the optimized configurations provide solutions for remote or critical infrastructure, such as hospitals, telecom sites, and off-grid facilities. Batteries that balance specific capacity and volumetric stability ensure long-duration, low-maintenance storage, crucial for maintaining power reliability in settings where energy resilience is paramount. Overall, these optimization results serve as a valuable guide for advancing adaptable, efficient GESS solutions tailored to specific application demands.

This work has leveraged the power of AI to advance the field of energy storage technologies by predicting key performance metrics (specific capacity, volume change, and average voltage) of battery electrodes. Utilizing data from the materials project database, a comprehensive analysis was conducted using a range of machine learning models, including Decision Tree, Random Forest, Support Vector Machines, and Deep Neural Network (DNN). The results revealed the superior predictive capabilities of the DNN model across various battery parameters. Specifically, the DNN model demonstrated very high accuracy in predicting specific capacity and volume change, offering valuable insights into the underlying relationships and enabling the identification of optimal electrode materials tailored to specific application requirements. Furthermore, the DNN model’s proficiency in predicting voltage underscores its potential as a reliable tool for accelerating electrode material research and development. Building upon these predictive capabilities, the study then employed the Non-dominated Sorting Genetic Algorithm 2 (NSGA2) multi-objective optimization algorithm to further enhance the performance and sustainability of the battery electrodes. By balancing the trade-offs between volume and capacity, the optimization framework successfully identified Pareto-optimal solutions that offer a holistic approach to electrode design, integrating AI-driven insights with optimization strategies to guide material property adjustments. In summary, this sequential approach (from prediction to optimization) provides a comprehensive pathway for leveraging AI in accelerating the discovery and development of sustainable energy storage solutions. Further work is needed to advance the AI-driven development and optimization of SIB electrode materials through a multi-phase approach: immediate model refinement, mid-term material discovery, and long-term integration of AI in novel battery chemistries. Short-term efforts should focus on expanding datasets via experimental collaboration and refining model architecture to enhance predictive accuracy. Incorporating ensemble methods and optimizing hyperparameters can further boost model performance, while validation with external datasets and real-world testing will solidify robustness. Parallel experimental studies are crucial for rapid prototyping, facilitating a feedback loop between laboratory results and model developments.

Additionally, while the current model does not include explicit descriptors for the ionic radius of the working ion or its coordination environment, we recognize their importance in determining intercalation dynamics and electrode stability. Future extensions of this work will focus on incorporating such features, which could improve predictive fidelity and provide more direct guidance for structural design. Another important direction is the incorporation of reversibility or cycling stability as a target property. While such data is currently scarce in computational repositories, future integration of experimental datasets could enable AI-based modeling of capacity retention, offering valuable insights for screening durable and robust electrode candidates. This framework can also be extended to predict additional performance metrics such as rate capability and cycle life. These additions would provide a more complete understanding of material suitability for practical SIB applications.

To optimize efficiency, advanced algorithms like particle swarm optimization (PSO) should complement the NSGA2, broadening objectives to include cost, environmental impact, and scalability. In the mid-term, enhanced models can drive the discovery of high-performance SIB materials, creating a comprehensive database and enabling pilot synthesis projects. Life-cycle assessment (LCA) will assess environmental impacts, guiding sustainable material selection.
A key long-term objective is to develop a sophisticated AI model that can predict optimal material compositions, supported by continual experimental validation.

Sara Alzaabi: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Ali Elkamel: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization. Georgios N. Karanikolos: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Ali Alhammadi: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Khalifa University of Science and Technology through the project number RC2-2018-024. The authors would also like to acknowledge the significant contributions of Khalifa University’s high-performance computing and research computing facilities. These resources have been crucial in obtaining the results presented in this research.
